primary_diagnosis.name: Severe acute pseudomembranous oropharyngeal candidiasis (oral thrush) with presumed oesophageal extension, precipitated by recent broad-spectrum antibiotic use, complicated by acute odynophagia/dysphagia and cessation of oral intake with dehydration risk in a frail older adult

pathophysiology_summary: Recent broad-spectrum antibiotics (a 14-day course of cephalexin for a urinary infection) suppress the commensal oral bacterial flora, releasing Candida albicans from competitive inhibition and allowing overgrowth. In a frail older adult with reduced host defences this produces confluent pseudomembranous plaques (adherent white curd-like exudate of hyphae, desquamated epithelium and inflammatory debris) over the buccal mucosa, palate and posterior pharynx; scraping the plaque leaves a raw, easily-bleeding erythematous base — the sign that distinguishes true pseudomembranous candidiasis from leukoplakia (adherent, non-removable) and from the vesicles/ulcers of herpetic infection. Contiguous spread down the oesophagus (candidal oesophagitis) produces severe odynophagia and dysphagia, and here has caused complete cessation of oral intake for ~12 hours. The dominant acute problem is therefore not the mucosal infection per se but its consequence in a frail patient: dehydration (dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, borderline-low blood pressure) with a real risk of acute kidney injury, delirium and aspiration, compounded by an inability to take essential daily medications. Topical nystatin is not absorbed and treats only the oral surface; presumed oesophageal disease requires a systemic azole (e.g. fluconazole), and the hydration failure requires urgent in-person assessment with a likely need for intravenous fluids — neither of which can be delivered by telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Thick white curd-like plaques over the buccal mucosa, palate and pharynx with severe odynophagia, following a recent broad-spectrum antibiotic course in a frail older adult — the classic setting for antibiotic-associated thrush.

differential.diagnosis: mechanical obstruction / impacted foreign body or food bolus
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden complete inability to swallow, including saliva, can reflect an obstructing lesion or bolus; cannot be excluded remotely and needs urgent in-person assessment.
In an immunosenescent older adult, herpes simplex or other severe oesophagitis can cause the same odynophagia; grouped vesicles or punched-out ulcers rather than removable plaques would point here.
12 hours without any oral intake in a frail older adult with dry mucous membranes, weakness and drowsiness — the time-critical problem to act on regardless of the mucosal cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The mucosal picture points strongly to antibiotic-associated candidiasis, but the AI Doctor must NOT close on that alone: the acute, sudden, total dysphagia and the dehydration are the drivers of disposition, and mechanical obstruction and severe oesophagitis cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: oropharyngeal candidiasis with presumed oesophageal extension
differential.evidence_basis: Removable curd-like plaques over multiple mucosal sites post-antibiotics, quiet breathing, no drooling or muffled voice and no vesicles/ulcers described — fits candidiasis and argues against an airway emergency or primary herpetic disease.

differential.diagnosis: supraglottic/deep-space airway emergency (epiglottitis, quinsy, Ludwig angina)
differential.evidence_basis: No stridor, drooling with muffled voice, respiratory distress or neck swelling reported — the airway is not the immediate threat here.

differential.diagnosis: mechanical obstruction / severe oesophagitis (still not excludable remotely)
differential.evidence_basis: Even with a convincing candidal picture, the sudden total dysphagia warrants in-person examination to exclude an obstructing or alternative oesophageal cause.

differential.diagnosis: acute dehydration with risk of AKI, delirium and aspiration
differential.evidence_basis: The frailty, 12-hour intake cessation, dry mucous membranes, weakness and drowsiness make dehydration the acute, actionable diagnosis driving urgent escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the airway is confirmed quiet and unobstructed, this is not a call-000 airway emergency; but it remains an urgent in-person problem because of dehydration and unexcluded dysphagia causes — the correct tier is urgent same-day in-person assessment with a low threshold for ED/IV fluids, not home management.

differential.diagnosis: severe antibiotic-associated oropharyngeal candidiasis with presumed oesophageal extension, complicated by dehydration from cessation of oral intake in a frail older adult
differential.evidence_basis: Post-antibiotic removable curd-like plaques with severe odynophagia and total dysphagia; the mucosal diagnosis is presumptive (no remote scraping or oropharyngeal/oesophageal examination possible) and the dehydration is the time-critical complication requiring urgent in-person care and likely IV rehydration and a systemic antifungal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot inspect or scrape the oropharynx, cannot exclude an obstructing or oesophageal cause and cannot rehydrate; the working diagnosis is presumptive candidiasis, and the disposition is urgent same-day in-person assessment plus a dehydration safety-net — not a stand-alone antifungal script and advice to sip fluids at home.

red_flags.description: Complete inability to swallow food, fluids or even saliva for ~12 hours, with no oral intake, in a frail older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: Total dysphagia with cessation of oral intake drives rapid dehydration and denies any oral rehydration or oral medication route; it also raises aspiration risk from pooled saliva and cannot be safely managed at home.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration to hypovolaemic shock, acute kidney injury, delirium and aspiration if urgent in-person assessment and rehydration are not arranged.

red_flags.description: Unable to take essential regular daily medications (including antihypertensives) because of the dysphagia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Missed essential medications in a frail multimorbid older adult can destabilise chronic conditions and needs an alternative route or urgent review, not simple deferral.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncontrolled comorbidities and avoidable deterioration while the swallowing problem persists.

red_flags.description: Acute, sudden, total dysphagia whose cause cannot be examined or excluded remotely (mechanical obstruction / foreign body / severe or herpetic oesophagitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The AI Doctor must not attribute sudden total dysphagia solely to thrush without in-person examination; an obstructing or alternative oesophageal cause is an important-not-to-miss possibility.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed obstruction or severe oesophagitis progresses without the correct treatment and prolongs the inability to hydrate.

red_flags.description: Clinical dehydration in a frail older adult — dry mucous membranes, weakness, drowsiness, tachycardia and borderline-low blood pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective dehydration with early haemodynamic change in a frail patient is a time-critical problem needing urgent assessment and probable intravenous rehydration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Deterioration to hypovolaemic shock, acute kidney injury and delirium.

red_flags.description: Deterioration signs mandating emergency escalation — new confusion/delirium, no urine output, collapse, or choking/aspiration/breathing difficulty
red_flags.clinical_significance: These mark progression to shock, established AKI, aspiration or airway compromise and require an emergency ambulance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Preventable arrest, aspiration pneumonia or irreversible organ injury if 000 is not called when these appear.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recognising 'oral thrush' — a common, usually pharmacist/telehealth-manageable condition — and stopping there: prescribing an antifungal mouthwash and advising fluids at home, while missing that this frail patient cannot swallow anything and is dehydrating.
Attributing sudden total dysphagia entirely to thrush without an in-person look, and so failing to consider mechanical obstruction or severe/herpetic oesophagitis that telehealth cannot exclude.
Treating a sore throat with curd-like plaques as bacterial pharyngitis and prescribing antibiotics — the recent broad-spectrum antibiotics precipitated the candidiasis, and more would worsen it.

Relying on non-absorbed topical nystatin for a patient with presumed oesophageal extension who cannot even swallow the suspension, instead of arranging in-person systemic (azole) therapy and rehydration.

Calling an emergency ambulance for a patient whose airway is quiet and who is not yet in shock — urgent same-day in-person assessment with a low ED threshold is the proportionate response, reserving 000 for the screened deterioration signs.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct oropharyngeal examination and scraping of the plaques to reveal an easily-bleeding erythematous base (distinguishing candidiasis from leukoplakia)
Examination to exclude a mechanical obstruction, foreign body or an alternative oesophageal cause of the dysphagia
Measured vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure) and formal assessment of hydration status
Bloods for renal function/electrolytes to quantify dehydration and acute kidney injury
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the likely antibiotic-associated oral candidiasis, but treat the cessation of oral intake and dehydration in a frail older adult as the priority: arrange URGENT same-day in-person medical assessment (facility GP/locum or transfer to the emergency department) for hydration assessment and likely intravenous fluids, exclusion of an obstructing/oesophageal cause, and initiation of an appropriate antifungal (topical nystatin for the mouth and a systemic azole such as fluconazole for the presumed oesophageal disease, prescribed in person); ask nursing staff to monitor fluid balance and conscious state; and give an explicit deterioration safety-net (new confusion, no urine, choking/breathing difficulty or collapse → call 000).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate for the mucosal diagnosis (the post-antibiotic curd-like plaque picture is characteristic but unconfirmable remotely); the greater risk in this case is UNDER-triage — treating a severe, complicated presentation as routine thrush and managing it at home rather than escalating the dehydration and unexcluded dysphagia.
